findings.description: The PDF presents the data as a compact wide table fitting on single pages, while the HTML breaks the table into a much taller, narrower layout with wrapped cell content, fundamentally changing the visual structure from a wide spreadsheet-like layout to a narrow multi-line row layout.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 shows a wide table with ~12 columns fitting horizontally. HTML page 1-2 shows the same table but with Owner-Facility column text wrapping extensively, making each row much taller and the table spanning multiple pages.

findings.description: The PDF table on page 2 for case LV-2012-0018 shows Total Penalties as $302.91, and the HTML shows $302.91 - data is consistent. However, the table's visual compactness is lost due to excessive row height from text wrapping in the Owner-Facility column.
findings.evidence: PDF has compact single-line or two-line rows; HTML has rows spanning 3-6 lines due to narrow column widths causing text wrapping.

findings.description: Nested <main> elements in the HTML. The document has a <main> wrapping element and then another <main> inside it containing the table. This is a structural issue but does not affect content fidelity.
findings.evidence:

findings.description: The title 'DWQ January 2012 Civil Penalty Assessments' appears once as h1 inside the page-header div. The PDF shows this title at the top of both pages, but consolidation of repeated page headers is acceptable.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 and page 2 both show 'DWQ January 2012 Civil Penalty Assessments' as a header. HTML shows it once as h1.
